parentQuestion: Preganglionic fibers of BOTH sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions secrete acetylcholine (ACh).

scaffolds.q: The ANS uses a two-neuron chain: Preganglionic and postganglionic.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Both sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions use two neurons in series - preganglionic (CNS to ganglion) and postganglionic (ganglion to target).
Correct! ACh was the first neurotransmitter discovered. It's used at neuromuscular junctions, autonomic ganglia, and parasympathetic effectors.

scaffolds.q: Preganglionic neurons synapse in autonomic ganglia.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The preganglionic neuron's axon travels from the CNS to a ganglion, where it synapses with the postganglionic neuron.

scaffolds.q: ALL preganglionic neurons - both sympathetic and parasympathetic - release ACh.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is universal - no exceptions. Every preganglionic fiber, regardless of division, releases ACh.

scaffolds.q: The sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions differ at the POSTGANGLIONIC level.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Preganglionic = same (ACh). Postganglionic = different (parasympathetic uses ACh, sympathetic mostly uses norepinephrine).

scaffolds.q: Nicotinic receptors in ganglia respond to ACh from preganglionic neurons.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Preganglionic ACh binds nicotinic receptors on postganglionic neurons, activating them.

scaffolds.q: The first neuron in BOTH divisions is cholinergic (uses ACh).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! 'Cholinergic' means ACh-releasing. All preganglionic neurons are cholinergic - a beautiful consistency in the ANS.

scaffolds.q: Both sympathetic and parasympathetic preganglionic fibers release ACh.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is one of the key principles of ANS organization. The divisions share the same preganglionic neurotransmitter.

scaffolds.q: The statement is TRUE - ALL preganglionic fibers secrete ACh.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is TRUE. Whether sympathetic or parasympathetic, every preganglionic neuron releases acetylcholine onto nicotinic receptors in the ganglion.
